did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model, a deep learning technique, to classify skin lesion symmetry from dermoscopic images into three classes ('fully asymmetric', 'symmetric with respect to one axis', or 'symmetric with respect to two axes'). It also evaluates transfer learning from pre-trained CNNs and introduces the SymDerm dataset for this task.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work, including the introduction of a new dataset (SymDerm with 615 skin lesions), the development of a novel CNN-based classification pipeline, and the evaluation of transfer learning versus traditional methods. It reports specific quantitative results (e.g., weighted-average F1-score of 64.5%) and compares them against other methods, which is characteristic of a research study rather than a review.

title: A novel approach for skin lesion symmetry classification with a deep learning model
brief_summary: This study addresses the task of skin lesion symmetry classification to aid in the early diagnosis of skin cancer. The authors propose a novel deep learning approach using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify dermoscopic images into three symmetry classes: fully asymmetric, symmetric with respect to one axis, or symmetric with respect to two axes. To support this task, they introduce the SymDerm dataset, comprising 615 expert-labeled skin lesions. The experimental framework evaluates the proposed CNN against traditional computer vision methods and transfer learning from pre-trained networks. The results demonstrate that the proposed simple, robust, and fast classification pipeline outperforms these alternative methods, achieving a weighted-average F1-score of 64.5%.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study classifies the symmetry of general skin lesions found in the SymDerm dataset (615 lesions) and references the PH2 dataset. The task is symmetry classification rather than specific histopathological diagnosis, though the context mentions melanoma, nevus, and basal cell carcinoma as examples of lesions where symmetry is relevant.

Mentioned in the introduction and related work as a malignant skin lesion characterized by asymmetry and as a class in referenced studies (e.g., Gutkowicz-Krusin et al., Ali et al.) but not as a specific label in the authors' own SymDerm dataset or primary classification task.

Mentioned in the introduction and related work as a benign or atypical lesion type often compared with melanoma regarding symmetry, but not as a specific label in the authors' own SymDerm dataset or primary classification task.
Mentioned in the related work section (Lorentzen et al.) as a lesion type that showed a higher degree of asymmetry than other pigmented skin lesions, except for malignant melanoma.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion symmetry class (fully asymmetric, symmetric with respect to one axis, or symmetric with respect to two axes)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly addresses the task of skin lesion symmetry classification using a CNN model. It classifies dermoscopic images into three discrete classes: 'fully asymmetric', 'symmetric with respect to one axis', and 'symmetric with respect to two axes'. The study also evaluates a binary classification variant (symmetric vs. asymmetric) and reports classification metrics such as weighted-average F1-score, Precision, Recall, and Balanced Accuracy.

datasets_used.name: SymDerm
datasets_used.description: A new dataset introduced by the authors for skin lesion symmetry classification, consisting of 615 expert-labeled skin lesions (mostly dermoscopic, some macroscopic) extracted from publicly available datasets (EDRA2002, ISIC, dermis, dermquest). Labels were assigned by three expert dermatologists using a max-voting method.

symmetric with respect to two axes

use.testing: Used for testing the proposed method and comparing with Toureau et al. method.
use.other: Used for domain adaptation evaluation on the PH2 dataset.

datasets_used.name: PH2
datasets_used.description: A public dataset containing 200 dermoscopic images with expert-annotated symmetry labels (completely symmetric, asymmetric with respect to one axis, or asymmetric with respect to two axes). The authors used these images to generate 438 augmented samples by adding simulated hair artifacts.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the classification performance of the proposed method and other setups, reporting mean and standard deviation of metrics.
To measure the degree of agreement between true values and predicted values over and beyond chance agreements.
To account for class imbalance by calculating metrics for each class and averaging them weighted by the number of true instances of each label.
To report the variability of performance metrics obtained across the 5-fold cross-validation folds.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance comparisons were made using descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation) and direct metric values (Balanced Accuracy, Kappa score, Precision, Recall, F1-score) derived from 5-fold stratified cross-validation.

SymDerm.experiment: Binary classification (Asymmetric vs. Symmetric) (5-fold stratified cross-validation)
SymDerm.notes: The proposed method substantially improves results over Stoecker et al. and Ali et al. in the binary setting.

Toureau et al. method.details: Neither the proposed method nor the Toureau et al. method could learn necessary features to classify data from the PH2 database when trained on SymDerm.

Proposed method.details: Neither the proposed method nor the Toureau et al. method could learn necessary features to classify data from the PH2 database when trained on SymDerm.

PH2.notes: The paper notes that dermatologists labeling lesions in different datasets may have different criteria, leading to poor generalization.

architecture_used.main_model: Proposed CNN (custom architecture inspired by VGG16 but simplified)
architecture_used.pretraining: Evaluated transfer learning from pre-trained CNNs (VGG16, ResNet50, Inception V3) on ImageNet, but the primary proposed model was trained from scratch.

architecture_details.layers: 10 layers total. Input layer (resize to 256x256x3), followed by 3 blocks. Each block contains one 3x3 convolution layer and one down-sampling layer (two-stride 3x3 convolution). Filters per block: 8, 16, and 32 respectively. Followed by two dense layers reducing features from 32768 to 128, then to 8. Final Softmax layer for 3-class classification.
architecture_details.design_basis: Based on VGG16 but with fewer layers and parameters to suit the small dataset size.

Max-voting method for label aggregation (annotation preprocessing)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Realistic hair artifacts were introduced to images from the PH2 dataset to generate variations of the same lesions while preserving the original symmetry annotation. This technique enlarged the PH2 dataset from 200 to 438 annotated samples.
Data augmentation restricted to flips in both horizontal and vertical directions during the training phase to improve generalization without distorting the shape of the lesion.

conclusions: The proposed simple, robust, and fast CNN-based classification pipeline outperforms traditional computer vision methods and transfer learning from pre-trained networks for skin lesion symmetry classification.
The proposed method achieved a weighted-average F1-score of 64.5% for the three-class symmetry classification task and 71.8% for the binary classification task.

The proposed method substantially improves upon traditional methods such as those by Stoecker et al., Ali et al., and Toureau et al., particularly in the binary classification setting where it achieved a Balanced Accuracy of 71.9%.
Transfer learning from pre-trained CNNs (VGG16, ResNet50, Inception V3) did not yield superior results compared to the proposed custom CNN trained from scratch on the SymDerm dataset.
The performance of the proposed method for the three-class problem is limited by the discrepancy among expert dermatologists in labeling symmetry.

The proposed method and the Toureau et al. method failed to generalize to the PH2 dataset when trained on SymDerm, suggesting that differences in labeling criteria between datasets hinder generalization.
The study introduces the SymDerm dataset, comprising 615 expert-labeled skin lesions, to support research in skin lesion symmetry classification.
important_remarks: The study introduces the SymDerm dataset with only 615 expert-labeled lesions, which is a small sample size for deep learning, potentially limiting the robustness and generalizability of the model.
The model failed to generalize to the external PH2 dataset when trained on SymDerm, indicating significant domain shift or labeling discrepancies between datasets.
There is notable subjectivity and inter-observer variability among the three expert dermatologists who labeled the SymDerm dataset, which limits the upper bound of classification performance.

Transfer learning from pre-trained CNNs (VGG16, ResNet50, Inception V3) did not outperform the custom CNN trained from scratch, suggesting that pre-trained features may not be optimal for this specific symmetry task.

The proposed CNN architecture is very simple (10 layers) with few parameters, which may be insufficient to capture complex features compared to deeper architectures, although it performed well on this specific task.
The presence of black frames in the dermoscopic images attracts the network's attention and may lead to errors, as noted in the Grad-CAM analysis.
The study relies on a max-voting method to resolve label disagreements among experts, which may not fully capture the uncertainty or nuance in symmetry assessment.
The dataset includes both dermoscopic and macroscopic images, introducing heterogeneity that could affect model performance if not properly accounted for.
